title: GPU inference memory leak risk in 72-hour soak—AI narrative service requires long-duration testing
component: AI inference service (LLM-backed narrative flagging, vLLM/TensorRT model serving)
reasoning: Ada capacity analysis (NFR-CAP-WB-001, NFR-CAP-WB-003) identifies 'AI model serving: memory leaks in GPU inference over long runs' as specific risk. LLM model serving frameworks (vLLM, TensorRT) can accumulate memory if token buffers are not properly freed between inferences or if prompt cache is not evicted. Liskov scalability (NFR-SCL-WB-003) budgets 16-40 GB GPU memory per instance; at 85% utilization (13.6-34 GB occupied), thermal throttling and performance degradation begin. Long-duration soak (72 hours at 1x peak load) with continuous narrative inference requests (80+ concurrent narratives queued) will expose memory leaks that daily smoke tests miss. Symptom: GPU memory watermark drifts upward linearly over 72h; p95 inference latency increases (queue backlog grows); AI narrative delivery SLA misses (Noyce NFR-LAT-007 budgets 30 min p95).
recommendation: Mandatory 72-hour soak test (Gate 5) with GPU memory instrumentation. Collect nvidia-smi metrics every 5 seconds; plot GPU memory trend (target: flat line after initial ramp-up, acceptable 5% variance). If linear drift detected, enable core dumps and use memory profiler (valgrind, NVIDIA Nsight) to identify leak source. Mitigation: (1) Implement periodic model server restart (hourly, during low-load windows); (2) Add aggressive prompt cache eviction (LRU with 1-hour TTL); (3) Pre-load model into GPU memory and pin it (avoid fragmentation from load/unload cycles).

title: Evidence store metadata index degradation—object listing latency at 10M objects requires indexing strategy validation
component: Evidence store (S3-like object storage) metadata query layer; database object inventory index
reasoning: Ada capacity (NFR-CAP-WB-005) explicitly identifies 'slow growth of object metadata index causing search degradation' as a known risk. By 2028, evidence store will contain 10M+ objects (140 GB cumulative, ~20 MB per case, 7,000 cases). Evidence search (Noyce NFR-LAT-017) budgets p95 <5s across 10-year archive. Object listing (enumerating objects by case, date, classification tier) is a foundational query; if index fragmentation grows, latency can degrade to 10-20s, breaking interactive workload. Risk is highest at 3-year mark (36-month projection: 140 GB, 7,000 cases). Volume test (Gate 6) must populate full 10-year projection and measure index behavior.
recommendation: Volume test (Gate 6) must execute against 140 GB replica with 10M objects. Benchmark: (1) Object listing by case_id (expected <100ms). (2) Object search by keyword (expected p95 <5s per Noyce). (3) Index statistics query (count objects by classification tier). Capture index size and page count before/after test. Monitor: query plan cost (EXPLAIN ANALYZE). If latency degrades >10% from baseline, implement indexing mitigation: (A) Partition evidence store by classification tier (separate S3 buckets: s3://caa-wb-official, s3://caa-wb-official-sensitive, etc.) to reduce per-partition object count; (B) Implement secondary metadata cache (Redis) for hot-path queries (recent cases); (C) Archive evidence older than 5 years to cold storage (Glacier) and rebuild index on remaining 5-year subset.

title: Swarm consensus O(n²) latency ceiling—20-vehicle swarm exceeds 500ms DAA resolution SLA
component: Swarm consensus protocol (pairwise de-confliction, DAA encounter resolution); onboard autonomy state machine
reasoning: Liskov scalability (NFR-SCL-SWM-002) identifies swarm consensus as O(n²) in vehicle count. For 5-vehicle swarm: 10 pairwise comparisons, ~50ms compute. For 10-vehicle swarm: 45 comparisons, ~200ms. For 20-vehicle swarm: 190 comparisons, ~800ms (exceeds 500ms latency budget per Noyce NFR-LAT-012 'DAA encounter detection and response ... within 5 s of first detection'). Liskov explicitly flags: '20-vehicle swarm → 190 pairwise comparisons, ~800ms compute (exceeds 500 ms SLA; sequential resolution breaks latency budget).' Current trials (Protector RG Mk1) are 5-vehicle baseline; Project Alvina ramps to 7-10 vehicles; GCAP/Tempest advanced autonomy may reach 20 vehicles. No swarm trial has validated 20-vehicle consensus latency; performance is unknown. Risk: encounter resolution latency spike → collision avoidance failure → safety event.
recommendation: Breakpoint test (component design input, not release gate) must evaluate consensus latency vs swarm size: simulate 5, 10, 15, 20 vehicle swarms with DAA encounter injection. Measure: time-to-resolution for coordinated maneuver decision. Determine: at what vehicle count does latency exceed SLA? If 20-vehicle consensus latency >500ms, mitigation is required: (A) Hierarchical clustering: partition 20-vehicle swarm into two 10-vehicle sub-swarms; each sub-swarm consensus internally (~200ms), then parent consensus between cluster representatives (~50ms) = 250ms total (within SLA). (B) Distributed decision-making: each aircraft pair independently decides maneuver (no global consensus), relies on local information + inter-aircraft coordination. (C) Accept degradation: if 20-vehicle trials are rare (GCAP/Tempest far-future), waive SLA for 20-vehicle scenarios and document operational constraint ('Swarm size limited to <15 vehicles for guaranteed <500ms encounter resolution').

title: Gate 4 (Stress) missing acceptance criteria for panel session shedding under overload—must define graceful degradation behavior
component: Panel session management, classification boundary routing, concurrent-user capacity
reasoning: Ada capacity (NFR-CAP-WB-003) defines peak panel concurrency: 20 users during plenary vote sessions (8-20 concurrent panelists per panel, 1-3 simultaneous panels = 15-20 peak). Stress gate (Gate 4, NFR-END-WB-002) requires graceful degradation at 2x peak (40+ concurrent users). Current test strategy specifies 'Panel-mode session overflow: shed at session creation (reject new panel open), not mid-session.' But acceptance criteria is vague: what is 'graceful shedding'? Acceptable p95 latency during shedding? Allowable rejection rate? This creates test ambiguity. Alan availability (NFR-AVL-W002) budgets 5-minute RTO for active-active failover; implies resilience but does not define overload behavior. Without clear acceptance criteria, test pass/fail is subjective.
recommendation: Refine Gate 4 acceptance criteria: (A) Define capacity limit: 'System supports max 20 concurrent panel members active-active. At 21+ concurrent members, new panel-open requests are rejected with HTTP 503 Service Unavailable + user-friendly message: "This panel is at capacity. Please try again in 5 minutes or contact CAA support."' (B) Define latency during overload: 'p95 latency for existing panel members (already logged in) must remain <10s p95 even during overload rejection phase.' (C) Define recovery: 'Once load drops below 20 concurrent, new panel opens are accepted within 30 seconds.' (D) Test procedure: ramp to 25 concurrent panelists; verify new panel opens are rejected; existing panelists can continue work; measure p95 latency; verify recovery time. Document results in Gate 4 release report.

title: Gate 3 (Load) missing duration and consistency-variance acceptance criteria—1-hour load test insufficient for 72h mission SLA
component: Applicant intake, inspector review, evidence processing pipelines
reasoning: Doneness criteria (Gate 3 - Load): 'critical journeys pass p95 budget at 1x peak for 1h'. One-hour duration is insufficient for long-duration workflow validation. Applicant submission lifecycle (REQ016, ada-capacity.json) is 8-12 weeks; a 1-hour load test exercises only initial intake phase (workspace creation, evidence upload, quick-gate processing). Missing: (A) Multiple upload cycles (day 1, day 3, day 7, day 20) to detect degradation during multi-week submission lifecycle. (B) Inspector review patterns (async over days/weeks); 1-hour test does not exercise concurrent applicant+inspector workflows. (C) Panel deliberation (5-10 day event); 1-hour test cannot validate panel session stability across deliberation lifecycle. Noyce SLA for decision-record drafting (end-to-end <30 min p95 per test strategy NFR-END-WB-008) is not validated by 1-hour load test.
recommendation: Extend Gate 3 duration and add acceptance criteria: (A) Duration: minimum 8 hours load test at 1x peak, simulating 1-week of operational load in compressed time. (B) Consistency-variance: 'p95 latency variance across 3 independent 1-hour run windows must be <10% (e.g., if run 1 is 3.0s, run 2 is 3.1s, run 3 is 2.95s; coefficient of variation <10%).' (C) Multi-user journeys: '3+ concurrent applicants each completing full Phase 1 workflow (upload → readiness check → submission) during test; 100% success rate required.' (D) Measurement: aggregate p95 over 8-hour window from running 5-min percentile windows (avoids statistical artifacts from short observation windows). Document Gate 3 results with latency histogram + variance analysis.

title: Gate 2 (Integration) missing multi-journey cross-functional scenarios—single-journey testing inadequate for multi-user interactions
component: Applicant intake + inspector review + panel deliberation pipelines (cross-journey interactions)
reasoning: Gate 2 (Integration) specifies: 'critical journeys pass p95 budget at 1x load with 3-run variance <10%'. Liskov scalability identifies separate journeys: applicant intake (Noyce NFR-LAT-001 through -005), inspector review (Noyce NFR-LAT-006, -008, -007), panel deliberation (Noyce NFR-LAT-009, -010). Gate 2 tests each journey in isolation (e.g., applicant journey 3 consecutive times, measure variance). Missing: concurrent applicant + inspector + panel workflows. Example: while applicant uploads evidence to Case A, inspector opens Case B for review, panel deliberates Case C. Contention on shared resources (database connection pool, evidence store, audit log ledger) could cause latency cross-talk between journeys. Single-journey integration testing does not expose cross-journey latency coupling.
recommendation: Enhance Gate 2 acceptance criteria: (A) Add multi-journey concurrent scenario: '3 concurrent workflows: applicant uploads 100 MB pack to Case 1, inspector reviews 5 evidence sections in Case 2 (Noyce NFR-LAT-008), panel action logs 10 comments to Case 3. All three workflows execute simultaneously. Measure: p95 latency for each journey isolated and concurrent. Acceptance: concurrent p95 latencies do not exceed isolated baselines by >10%.' (B) Repeat 3-run consistency variance check on concurrent scenario (not just isolated journeys). (C) Measure resource contention: database connection pool utilization, evidence store PUT/GET latency histograms. Document: any observed latency coupling (e.g., applicant upload slowed by inspector queries) and mitigation (e.g., read replicas for inspector queries, separate connection pools).

title: Gate 1 (Unit) missing performance budget inheritance from Liskov scalability—GPU inference budgets not validated in unit tests
component: GPU inference components (coverage matrix OCR, semantic classification, AI narrative LLM); unit test harness
reasoning: Gate 1 (Unit) specifies: 'new performance-sensitive functions have unit perf tests with budgets'. Liskov scalability (NFR-SCL-WB-002, NFR-SCL-WB-003) identifies two GPU-intensive components: (A) Coverage matrix engine (OCR 180s budget, semantic classification 200s, matrix computation 90s per Noyce NFR-LAT-003). (B) AI narrative service (chunk inference 900s total per Noyce NFR-LAT-007). These components have significant performance budgets; unit tests should validate that individual inference calls (OCR on single PDF page, semantic classification on single clause, LLM inference on single evidence chunk) meet sub-budgets. Current Gate 1 is vague: does it require unit tests for GPU inference functions? Unit test framework (JUnit, PyTest) may not have GPU-capable runners; testing GPU code typically requires container-based or cloud-based test infrastructure.
recommendation: Define Gate 1 GPU inference testing approach: (A) Create GPU-capable unit test suite (Docker container with nvidia-base image, CUDA 12.x, Jetson or A100 simulator). (B) Define performance budgets for unit-level operations: (i) OCR on single page: <1s per Noyce budget (OCR is 180s for ~180 page evidence pack). (ii) Semantic classification inference on single clause: <100ms (200s for ~2000 clauses). (iii) LLM inference on single chunk: <5s (900s for ~180 chunks). (C) Unit tests must measure latency histogram (p50, p90, p95) for each operation; assert p95 < budget. (D) Add memory profiling: GPU memory allocated during inference should be <1 GB for single inference call (catch memory leaks early). (E) Run unit tests as pre-commit hook in CI/CD pipeline; fail build if any unit perf test exceeds budget.

title: Audit log write latency drift risk—ledger fragmentation or fsync overhead can degrade 500ms p95 budget over 72 hours
component: Audit / deliberation log (immutable append-only distributed ledger, see Liskov NFR-SCL-WB-008)
reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-009 budgets 500ms p95 for panel-action log write (audit trail is regulatory-critical per REQ012). Liskov NFR-SCL-WB-008 describes audit log as sharded append-only ledger (Kafka or similar). Write performance depends on fsync latency (disk durability) and quorum ACK latency (replication consensus). On Liskov design: '1 shard (1 panel) handles ~50 actions/sec. Quorum write: 2-of-3 replicas ACK within 150 ms.' Over 72-hour soak with cumulative 10,000+ panel actions, ledger grows; if fsync overhead increases (disk cache pressure, file fragmentation), quorum ACK latency can drift from 150ms baseline to 250-300ms, pushing total write latency to 600-700ms (exceeds 500ms SLA). Symptom: p95 latency drifts linearly upward over 72h; panelists perceive 'saving comment is getting slow'.
recommendation: Soak test (Gate 5) must monitor audit log write latency histogram every 10 minutes. Plot trends: (A) Quorum ACK latency component (time for 2-of-3 replicas to ACK); target stable <150ms. (B) Total write latency p95; target stable <500ms. If drift detected, investigate: (i) Disk I/O saturation (iostat, fio); (ii) fsync overhead increase (strace -e fsync); (iii) Replication lag (Kafka partition lag metrics). Mitigation: (A) Batch writes: aggregate 10-100 actions into single ledger entry (reduces write frequency by 10-100x, amortizes fsync overhead). (B) Async fsync: return write ACK to client before durability confirmation (acceptable for audit trail if fsync is durably queued and guaranteed to succeed). (C) Separate ledger shards per panel: distribute write load across multiple ledger instances (already in Liskov design).

title: C2 link loss detection latency—1-second SLA tight for RF fade detection during sustained operations
component: C2 link monitoring, loss-of-signal detection, autonomous survival failsafe (Noyce NFR-LAT-020)
reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-020 'Loss-of-C2 link detection and autonomous survival activation' budgets 1000ms hard deadline (p50 200ms, p99 1000ms). Vibe requirements (C2LinkResilience per alan-availability.json NFR-AVL-S002) budget 90+ seconds of autonomous survival before RTL triggered. Swarm operational testing must validate C2 loss detection latency in realistic RF conditions: (A) Gradual link fade (RSSI declining, packet loss increasing from 0% to 100% over 5-30 seconds). (B) Sudden link loss (RF jammed, operator loses ground-station connection). Endurance flight tests (4-hour duration per NFR-END-SWM-001) will encounter natural RF fades (geographic shadowing, multipath reflections). System must detect loss within 1 second and initiate autonomous failsafe (altitude reduction, formation hold, RTL) within SLA. Detection latency >1 second means aircraft are uncontrolled for >1 second before autonomous recovery activates—collision risk.
recommendation: Endurance flight test (NFR-END-SWM-001) must include controlled C2 loss scenario: (A) During sustained 4-hour flight, deliberately lose RF signal for 30-60 seconds (induce RF blackout via Faraday cage or jammer). (B) Measure: time from signal loss (ground station loses valid packet reception) to autonomous behavior initiation (aircraft begins altitude reduction per failsafe logic). Acceptance: detection latency <1000ms. (C) Verify: autonomous survival behavior holds altitude/formation for 90+ seconds, then initiates RTL. (D) Measure: recovery time after C2 link is restored (aircraft re-establish formation and resume commanded flight plan). Document: any transient errors or oscillations during link loss/restoration.
